title: Chicken and leek pie
subtitle: Leek, tarragon, and cream under a golden lid
excerpt: Chicken thighs with slow-cooked leeks, a little tarragon, and enough cream to make the sauce glossy, under a shortcrust or puff pastry lid. The gentler, more fragrant sibling of chicken and mushroom pie.
sourceNotes: British home-cooking classic with French influences (the leek-tarragon-cream combination draws on poulet à la crème). Documented in twentieth-century British cookery including Delia Smith and Jane Grigson's work. Method draws on the classic British chicken pie tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the filling for up to 3 months. Top with fresh pastry and bake from cold.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the filling up to 2 days ahead. Cold filling under pastry is the best approach.

content.text: Chicken and leek has a quieter flavour than chicken and mushroom: the leeks sweeten and collapse into the sauce, the tarragon adds aniseed warmth without pushing to the front, and the cream brings the whole thing together without being heavy. It suits shortcrust as readily as puff pastry; the firmer, crumblier lid can be a better match for the gentle filling.

Leeks need a longer cook than onions to fully soften; underdone leeks have a fibrous, slightly grassy quality that the finished pie will retain. Give them a full 10 minutes over low heat before combining with the chicken.

items.prepNote: boneless, skinless, cut into 3 cm pieces

content.text: Melt half the butter in a wide saucepan over medium-low heat. Add {{leek}} sliced leeks with a pinch of salt and cook for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until very soft but not coloured. Remove and set aside. Raise the heat to medium-high and, in the same pan with the remaining butter, brown {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thigh pieces lightly on both sides, about 2 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium and melt the remaining {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the pan. Add {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock, then {{double-cream}} of double cream, until smooth. Cook, stirring, until the sauce thickens to a heavy coating consistency. Stir in the mustard if using and the tarragon. Season well. Fold in the leeks and chicken pieces. Leave to cool.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Spoon the cold filling into a pie dish. Roll out the pastry to 4 mm thick and lay over the dish. Trim and press the edges to seal. Brush with beaten egg. Score the surface lightly and cut a steam vent in the centre. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is golden. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Leeks still stringy in the finished pie
items.cause: Not cooked long enough before being added to the sauce
items.fix: Leeks should be soft enough to squash between two fingers before they go into the filling. Cook for the full 10 to 12 minutes.

items.symptom: Sauce has a slight floury taste
items.cause: Roux was not cooked for long enough before the stock was added
items.fix: Stir the flour and butter together for a full 2 minutes over heat before adding any liquid.

items.cause: Egg wash was applied too thinly or the oven temperature was too low
items.fix: Brush a second coat of egg wash on after 15 minutes and return to the oven.

items.cause: More tarragon than specified, or the herb is very fresh and intense
items.fix: Halve the tarragon next time. Tarragon is the right herb here but the sauce should taste of chicken and leek first.

content.text: Chicken and leek is a pairing with roots in Welsh and Scottish cooking, where leeks have always been the default allium. The cream-enriched version that appears here draws on French influence: the leek-and-cream combination from poulet à la crème, adapted for the British pie tradition that demands a pastry lid rather than a finished sauce. It sits in the same family as chicken and mushroom pie but appeals to those who want a less earthy, more delicate result.
